role_summary: SNCC field secretary; treasurer and SNCC representative of the Baltimore Civic Interest Group (CIG); attended the July 1961 founding SNCC coordinating committee meeting in Louisville as CIG's representative; fall 1961 voter-registration organizer in McComb, Mississippi alongside Bob Moses, helping establish the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket; 1963 organizer in Cambridge, Maryland alongside Gloria Richardson.
ai_reading: Robinson's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a high cosine count of 336, the catalog's clearest single-organizer-pairing within the early SNCC-Mississippi cohort: Robinson's fall 1961 McComb work and McLaurin's 1962-1968 Sunflower County work both fall under the McComb-and-Mississippi field-secretary tradition, and the embedding catches that shared register as a stronger signal than either's pairing with the SNCC headquarters or with later post-1964 Freedom Summer voices. The novel observation his page surfaces is the cross-state-organizer signature: Robinson worked Baltimore, then Mississippi, then Cambridge Maryland in a three-region arc that the historiography often subordinates to single-region narratives; the embedding catches the field-secretary-traveling-organizer vocabulary as a coherent signal regardless of which state he was in at any given moment.
biographical_paragraph: While Robinson's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), his external biographical record is documented through the SNCC Digital Gateway and the institutional records of the Baltimore Civic Interest Group [src: 1, src: 2]. Robinson was born October 2, 1939 and raised in Baltimore, Maryland. He joined the Baltimore Civic Interest Group at the insistence of City Councilman Walter Dixon, became the group's treasurer, and represented CIG at the July 1961 SNCC coordinating committee meeting in Louisville, Kentucky [src: 2]. In fall 1961 he traveled to McComb, Mississippi to work voter registration alongside Bob Moses, helping establish the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket; he left Mississippi later that fall after white violence escalated, including the murder of fellow organizer Herbert Lee [src: 2]. He worked the desegregation campaigns along Route 40 between Washington, D.C. and New York City in the early 1960s and joined the 1963 Cambridge, Maryland organizing alongside Gloria Richardson [src: 2]. CIG registered nearly 10,000 Black voters in Baltimore during his tenure; he mentored Northern-college SNCC recruits including future Eyes on the Prize producer Judy Richardson (catalog entry 69, the same Robinson-mentee whose own page documents the Atlanta-and-Mississippi arc that followed) [src: 2].
movement_context: Robinson's fall 1961 McComb assignment placed him inside the first SNCC voter-registration project in the Mississippi Delta, the Pike County campaign Bob Moses had begun that July at the request of Amzie Moore and the NAACP's Mississippi State Conference [src: 3]. The voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket on Summit Street in McComb served as the operational base for the Pike, Walthall, and Amite County registration drives; SNCC field secretaries including Robinson, Moses, Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17), Marion Barry, Travis Britt, and John Hardy worked from that location through September and October 1961, walking rural Black residents to the Pike County Courthouse to attempt to register [src: 3, 4]. The September 25, 1961 murder of Amite County dairy farmer Herbert Lee by Mississippi state representative E. H. Hurst (an act for which Hurst was never indicted, and which preceded the 1964 reprisal murder of witness Louis Allen) marked the operational closing of the first McComb project; SNCC field staff withdrew from McComb through October and November 1961 as white-supremacist violence escalated beyond what the small field-secretary contingent could continue to operate within [src: 4]. After McComb, Robinson returned to Maryland, where the Baltimore Civic Interest Group's voter-registration drive in the early 1960s registered close to 10,000 Black voters in Baltimore; the CIG was the Mid-Atlantic urban-North counterpart to the Mississippi Delta rural-South work, and Robinson's institutional position as treasurer and SNCC representative made him one of the principal cross-border organizing bridges between the two project geographies [src: 2]. In 1963 Robinson worked the Cambridge, Maryland campaign alongside Gloria Richardson (catalog entry 49), the longest-running sustained civil-rights confrontation in the Mid-Atlantic in the early-1960s period, the campaign whose July 1963 Treaty of Cambridge Richardson refused to endorse at the U.S. Department of Justice; the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee Richardson chaired was the SNCC-affiliated organizing body on Maryland's Eastern Shore, and Robinson's presence in Cambridge connected the CNAC field operation to the wider SNCC project-county network [src: 4, 5]. Robinson married Betty Garman Robinson (catalog entry 7), the SNCC national office staff coordinator who ran the Friends of SNCC chapter network from the Atlanta headquarters under James Forman's direction; their marriage joined the field-secretary side of SNCC's institutional architecture to the fundraising-and-Northern-support side, and the two of them moved together to Baltimore in 1970 [src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: Robinson and Betty Garman Robinson moved to Baltimore in 1970 and organized in Black-and-white coalition work, education-equity advocacy, and housing-justice campaigns in Baltimore for half a century; Betty Garman Robinson's continuing-organizer record was anchored in the SNCC Legacy Project and the SNCC Digital Gateway documentary platform at Duke University, which holds Reginald Robinson's contemporary correspondence and oral-history materials alongside hers [src: 6]. The Baltimore organizing record the two of them built across the 1970s, 1980s, and 1990s connected the housing-justice work of the Baltimore-area coalition networks to the SNCC alumni network's continuing public-correspondence channels, and the SNCC Digital Gateway's biographical entry for Robinson treats his post-1970 Baltimore tenure as a continuous extension of the McComb-and-Cambridge field-secretary trajectory rather than as a separate retirement phase [src: 2]. After Betty Garman Robinson's death in October 2020, Reginald Robinson continued public-speaking work for the SNCC Legacy Project and related institutional-memory programs, contributing biographical and operational testimony to the SNCC Digital Gateway's voter-registration and Mississippi-project documentation streams [src: 2]. The Hands on the Freedom Plow memoir-anthology edited by Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner (University of Illinois Press, 2010) includes a Cambridge Movement chapter contribution from Reginald Robinson and others documenting the CNAC-SNCC working relationship from the field-secretary perspective; the volume is the principal institutional memoir-anthology in which his own first-person voice appears in published form alongside the broader women-in-SNCC institutional record [src: 7]. The Howell Raines 1977 oral-history volume My Soul Is Rested, drawing on interviews conducted in the mid-1970s, places Robinson's McComb 1961 record in the early contemporaneous documentary stream that predates the later 1980s and 1990s scholarly institutional histories by Carson and Dittmer; the Raines interviews and the SNCC Digital Gateway entry together form the principal published-source basis for the Robinson biographical record outside the LoC oral history itself [src: 8].

gallery.alt: Bob Moses, 1962 University of Texas Cactus yearbook portrait
gallery.caption: Bob Moses in a 1961 portrait that appeared in the 1962 Cactus yearbook (University of Texas at Austin, page 344). Moses was the SNCC field secretary who directed the McComb, Mississippi voter-registration project that summer and fall of 1961; Reginald Robinson joined the McComb operation alongside Moses, helping run the voter-registration school above the Burgland Supermarket on Summit Street through October 1961.
gallery.photographer: Unknown (yearbook portrait; not credited in The Cactus, 1962, page 344)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from The Cactus, University of Texas at Austin yearbook, 1962)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice or renewal)

gallery.alt: SNCC poster 'One Man, One Vote' (ca. 1963), photograph by Danny Lyon
gallery.caption: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee poster 'One Man, One Vote' from circa 1963, photograph by Danny Lyon (SNCC staff photographer). The 'One Man, One Vote' slogan framed the SNCC voter-registration program Robinson worked in McComb in fall 1961 and in the Baltimore Civic Interest Group registration drive that, by his account in the SNCC Digital Gateway record, registered close to 10,000 Black voters in Baltimore during his tenure as CIG treasurer and SNCC representative. Held by the Seattle Public Library and contributed to the Digital Public Library of America.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Seattle Public Library and the Digital Public Library of America)
gallery.license: No Known Copyright (RightsStatements.org designation; the holding institution reasonably believes no copyright or related rights restrict use)

gallery.alt: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964
gallery.caption: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964, two and a half years after Robinson's fall 1961 McComb work. The Mississippi voter-registration project Robinson helped seed at the Burgland Supermarket school in 1961 became, by 1964, the institutional anchor for the Council of Federated Organizations Freedom Summer program that brought roughly one thousand Northern student volunteers into the Mississippi Delta. Held by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

sources.title: Reginald Robinson oral history

sources.title: Reggie Robinson biographical entry

sources.title: Clayborne Carson, In Struggle: SNCC and the Black Awakening of the 1960s
sources.publisher: Harvard University Press, 1981 (Bancroft Prize 1982); WorldCat OCLC 3025953 (the standard institutional history of SNCC including the fall 1961 McComb voter-registration project, the Pike-Walthall-Amite county geography, and the institutional withdrawal following the Herbert Lee murder)

sources.title: John Dittmer, Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 1994 (Bancroft Prize 1995); WorldCat OCLC 26012597 (the standard scholarly history of the Mississippi civil rights movement including the McComb voter-registration project, Herbert Lee's September 25, 1961 murder by E. H. Hurst, and the 1964 reprisal murder of witness Louis Allen)

sources.title: Peter B. Levy, Civil War on Race Street: The Civil Rights Movement in Cambridge, Maryland
sources.publisher: University Press of Florida, 2003; WorldCat OCLC 51163879 (the standard scholarly institutional history of the Cambridge Movement, the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee, the SNCC field-secretary network including Robinson that worked the Cambridge campaign in 1963, and the Treaty of Cambridge negotiation)

sources.title: Betty Garman Robinson
sources.publisher: SNCC Digital Gateway, Duke University (documenting the Atlanta SNCC office tenure 1964-1966 and the 1970 move to Baltimore alongside Reginald Robinson, with continuing community-organizing record through 2020)

sources.title: Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, Dorothy M. Zellner, editors, Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 2010; WorldCat OCLC 863206155 (the institutional memoir-anthology of SNCC's women organizers, with a Cambridge Movement chapter referencing Reginald Robinson and others documenting the CNAC-SNCC working relationship from the field-secretary perspective)

sources.title: Howell Raines, My Soul Is Rested: Movement Days in the Deep South Remembered
sources.publisher: Putnam, 1977 (reprinted Penguin, 1983); WorldCat OCLC 3025950 (oral-history interviews with Mississippi Movement participants conducted in the mid-1970s, providing early contemporaneous documentary record of the McComb project geography Robinson worked in fall 1961)

sources.title: James Forman, The Making of Black Revolutionaries: A Personal Account
sources.publisher: Macmillan, 1972 (paperback University of Washington Press, 1997); WorldCat OCLC 4884116 (Forman's autobiographical institutional history of SNCC including the field-secretary network, the Mississippi project geography, and the inter-state organizing arcs that connected Baltimore CIG to McComb and Cambridge)
